slides.description: This slide, titled "AI Screenplays," serves as the introductory slide for the presentation. The image depicts a cartoon office scene, clearly inspired by the South Park animated series, featuring a character resembling Mr. Garrison standing behind a large desk. On the desk, several golden awards like Oscars and Emmys are visible, alongside a unique red award labeled "CARTMAN." Behind the character, a large window reveals a cityscape with the prominent "HOLLYWOOD" sign in the distance. To the right, the iconic 'AWESOM-O' robot costume, a known character from South Park, stands by the desk, while a framed picture on the wall amusingly declares "AWESOME-O LAME." Various documents, some clearly labeled "SCREENPLAY," are scattered on the desk, emphasizing the central theme of the presentation. This visual setup, combining elements of the entertainment industry with a satirical take on AI through the 'AWESOM-O' reference, directly sets the stage for a discussion on artificial intelligence's involvement in screenplay writing, likely exploring both its potential and perceived shortcomings.

This slide, titled "Generating Long Documents," continues the discussion from the introductory "AI Screenplays" slide by specifying the core capability being explored. It features four bullet points detailing how AI can be utilized in this context. The first point states that "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents," followed by examples like "Books, reports, screenplays," directly linking to the theme established by the previous slide. The third bullet clarifies the presentation's immediate goal: "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies." Finally, the slide notes that "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents," indicating the broader applicability of the concepts to be discussed, using screenplays as a concrete and relevant example to the overall topic.

This slide, titled "Challenge: \"One-Shot\" Long-Form," directly follows the previous slide's introduction of generating long documents like screenplays with AI. It pivots from the capability to the inherent difficulties, acting as a problem statement for the presentation's central theme. The first bullet point asserts that "It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot." The second main bullet, "Local vs global," elaborates on this challenge, explaining that while AI excels at writing small parts, it struggles to maintain coherence across an entire document. This issue is further detailed with sub-points: the AI "Becomes \"lost in the middle\"" and suffers from "Context drift," leading to hallucination or loss of focus deep into the document, thus setting the stage for why an "agentic flow" (as mentioned in Slide 2) is necessary to overcome these limitations.

This slide, titled "Movie Screenplays," elaborates on the structural complexity of screenplays, a direct follow-up to the challenges of generating long documents introduced in the prior slide. It highlights that screenplays comprise many different elements an AI must capture, including "Story shape," the "Goals of 'sequences' in the movie," and "Scene action and dialogue." Crucially, the slide then details the "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," breaking it down from a high-level "Premise" to a "Story arc," then to "Sequences," and finally to individual "Scenes." This breakdown into distinct, nested components directly addresses the "one-shot" generation challenge from Slide 3, suggesting that by understanding and generating these hierarchical elements separately, the AI can overcome issues of "context drift" and maintain coherence across the entire document, paving the way for the agentic flow mentioned in Slide 2.

This slide, titled "Screenplay Flow," presents a visual flowchart illustrating the proposed agentic process for generating a screenplay, directly expanding on the hierarchical structure introduced in Slide 4. The diagram details a sequential flow, starting with a "Premise" (blue box, premise.json), which then progresses to the "Arc" (purple box, arc.json). From the "Arc," the process branches to generate both "Sequences" (orange box, sequence.json) and "Scenes" (green box, scenes.json). This visual breakdown explicitly demonstrates how the complex task of long-form screenplay generation, identified as challenging in Slide 3, is managed by breaking it into distinct, interdependent modules, thus realizing the "agentic flow" solution mentioned in Slide 2. The inclusion of ".json" file names beneath each stage suggests that each step produces a structured data output, facilitating a coherent and modular approach to overcoming issues like context drift.

This slide, titled "Premise Agent," details the first component of the agentic screenplay generation flow introduced in Slide 5. It describes a chat agent designed to interactively discuss the core concept of a movie, covering essential elements like the title, logline, characters, genre, and central conflict, building upon the foundational "Premise" step of the hierarchical structure from Slide 4. The slide also highlights the agent's ability to offer plot ideas when the user faces creative blocks and notes that this initial brainstorming session, while interactive, can take a few minutes to fully specify the movie's major elements. Crucially, it states that this agent's output is a structured file named "premise.json," directly linking back to the visual flowchart in Slide 5 and providing the concrete output for the subsequent stages of screenplay generation, thus addressing the modular approach needed to overcome the "one-shot" generation challenge from Slide 3.

This slide, titled "Premise.json," directly illustrates the output of the Premise Agent described in the previous slide, serving as a concrete example of the initial structured data generated in the screenplay flow. It displays a JSON object containing key foundational elements for a movie, such as a "project_id," "title" ("Shadow of the Dark World"), and a "logline" that describes a heroic quest involving characters like Link, Princess Zelda, and the villain Ganon. The "format_notes" amusingly indicate that "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat," humorously linking back to the presentation's introductory theme. This structured output, also specifying "genre" (fantasy, action, adventure) and "themes," fulfills the role of the 'premise.json' file noted in the "Screenplay Flow" (Slide 5), demonstrating how the agentic approach breaks down complex generation into manageable, well-defined steps to overcome the "one-shot" long-form writing challenge introduced in Slide 3.

This slide, titled "Premise.json," continues to display the structured JSON output generated by the Premise Agent, building directly upon the example introduced in the previous slide. It presents further detailed foundational elements for the movie, including the "central_conflict" outlining Link's quest to prevent Ganon from destroying Hyrule. The "setting" is specified with a "primary_time" of "High fantasy era" and a list of "primary_places" like Hyrule Castle and Dark World. Additionally, the "protagonists" array details Link's name, his role as an "Unassuming hero," and his primary "want" to save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and stop Ganon. This continued display of the premise.json file reinforces how the agentic flow breaks down complex screenplay generation into manageable, detailed, and structured steps, directly addressing the "one-shot" long-form writing challenges by providing a robust and specific foundation for the subsequent stages of the narrative creation process.

This slide, titled "Premise.json," continues the detailed display of the structured JSON output from the Premise Agent, building upon the foundational elements shown in the previous two slides. It specifies key character details, including the protagonist "Link," detailing his role and specific want to prevent Ganon. Crucially, it introduces the "key_antagonist" as "Aghanim," outlining his role as Ganon's pawn and his goal to release Ganon, along with a "key_ally" named "Sahasrahla" and his role. Finally, the slide clearly defines the overarching "stakes" of the narrative: "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon." This further segmentation and detailed specification of characters and conflict within the premise.json file exemplifies how the agentic flow breaks down the complex screenplay generation into manageable, structured, and highly detailed components, addressing the "one-shot" long-form writing challenges by creating a robust story foundation.

This slide, titled "Arc Agent," details the subsequent component in the agentic screenplay generation process, immediately following the creation of the premise. It introduces a chat agent specifically designed to develop the movie's story arc, which the slide clarifies consists of multiple acts, directly building on the hierarchical structure introduced in Slide 4. A key feature is that this agent takes the previously generated `premise.json` file as input, ensuring a coherent narrative by utilizing the foundational context established in Slides 7-9. The user interacts with the agent to outline and draft each act sequentially, a process that directly addresses the "one-shot" long-form writing challenge by preventing context drift. The 'AWESOM-O' robot in the corner playfully links back to the presentation's introductory theme. Ultimately, this agent produces a structured output file named `arc.json`, aligning with the 'Arc' stage depicted in the "Screenplay Flow" from Slide 5.

This slide, titled "Arc.json," provides a concrete example of the structured JSON output generated by the Arc Agent, specifically detailing Act 3 of the movie "Shadow of the Dark World." Directly following the interactive act outlining described in the previous slide, this JSON object contains a comprehensive summary of the act's plot, a dramatic question driving its events, and a list of "must_hit_beats" for key narrative points, such as Link's confrontation with Aghanim. The inclusion of an "approx_pages_note" further emphasizes the modular breakdown of the screenplay. This detailed, structured output for a single act directly addresses the challenges of generating long, coherent documents in one shot (Slide 3) by creating a precise and segmented narrative blueprint for the overall story arc, as envisioned in the "Screenplay Flow" from Slide 5.

This slide, titled "Sequence Agent," introduces the next component in the hierarchical agentic flow for screenplay generation, directly following the Arc Agent (Slides 10-11) and the `arc.json` output (Slide 11). It details an automated agent, notably without a chat interface, responsible for writing the individual sequences within each act of the movie, building upon the "Sequences" stage from the "Screenplay Flow" (Slide 5) and the hierarchical structure outlined in Slide 4. The agent's inputs include `premise.json` and `arc.json` for a global understanding, along with all previously generated sequences for local context, which directly addresses the "context drift" challenge for long-form generation (Slide 3). The slide specifies the primary elements defined for each sequence: its narrative goal, key locations, and an exit beat describing how the sequence ends and transitions to the next. Consistent with the modular approach, this agent ultimately generates a structured output file named `sequence.json`. The 'AWESOM-O' robot in the top right corner maintains the presentation's overarching theme.

This slide, titled "Sequence.json," provides a concrete example of the structured output generated by the Sequence Agent, which was introduced in the previous slide. It displays a JSON object containing key details for the first sequence of the movie "Shadow of the Dark World," directly building upon the project's foundational premise and arc. The output includes the `project_id`, total `sequence_count`, and an array of sequence definitions. For the initial sequence, "Zelda's Urgent Plea," the JSON specifies its `id`, `act` (Act 1), narrative `goal` to establish Link's call to adventure, and an `emotional_tone`. It further details `key_locations` like "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)," along with an `exit_beat` that sets up Link's infiltration of the castle. This granular, structured output demonstrates how the agentic flow breaks down complex screenplay generation into manageable units, directly addressing the "one-shot" long-form writing challenge by preventing context drift and ensuring narrative coherence.

This slide, titled "Scene Agent," introduces the final and most granular automated component in the hierarchical screenplay generation flow, immediately following the Sequence Agent (Slide 12-13). It describes an agent, notably without a chat interface, that writes individual scenes for each sequence of the movie, directly addressing the 'Scenes' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" (Slide 5) and the hierarchical structure from Slide 4. The agent takes multiple inputs per sequence: `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and `sequence.json` provide a global view of the movie (as seen in Slides 7-9, 11, and 13 respectively), while all previously generated scenes within the current and previous sequences supply local context, directly preventing the "context drift" challenge identified in Slide 3. Primary scene elements defined include the "Slug line" (scene location) and "Blocks" (actions and dialogues). Ultimately, this agent creates a structured output file named `scenes.json`, consistent with the modular approach to generating long-form content.

This slide, titled "Scenes.json," provides a direct example of the structured output generated by the Scene Agent, which was introduced in the previous slide, representing the most granular level of the agentic screenplay generation flow. It displays a JSON object detailing the first scene of "Shadow of the Dark World," identified by "S0001" and linked to "SEQ1." The scene's slugline, "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT," sets the initial setting. The JSON breaks down the scene into a series of "blocks," alternating between "action" descriptions—such as Link sleeping, hearing a whisper, and jolting awake—and "dialogue," exemplified by Princess Zelda's urgent voice-over plea for help, mentioning "Aghanim." This concrete example demonstrates how the complex task of screenplay writing is meticulously segmented into individual actions and dialogue, ensuring narrative coherence and preventing context drift, directly addressing the "one-shot" long-form writing challenges by fulfilling the 'Scenes' stage in the overall "Screenplay Flow."

This slide, titled "Enhancement Agents," introduces a crucial refinement to the agentic screenplay generation flow established in previous slides. After detailing the generation of each component (premise, arc, sequences, and scenes) and their respective JSON outputs, this slide explains how to improve these intermediate outputs. The main bullet point states that "We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs," with sub-points suggesting ways to "Write better sequences or scenes," "Clean up the premise," and "Make the arc more creative." Visually, a diagram, similar to the "Screenplay Flow" from Slide 5, is presented, but with small robot icons labeled as 'AWESOM-O' style enhancement agents positioned between each stage (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes), indicating that these agents process and improve the `.json` output from one stage before it feeds into the next. A larger image of a gold robot labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" reinforces this concept, showing how additional automated steps can be integrated into the modular pipeline to elevate the quality of the generated long-form content.

This slide, titled "Generalizing to Other Documents," demonstrates the broader applicability of the agentic flow for long document generation developed in previous slides. A key bullet point highlights that "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents." A detailed table then illustrates how the core concepts of "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," and "Scene," which were meticulously defined for screenplays, can be adapted for other content. The table maps these stages to documents like "Book" (e.g., Arc becomes "Table of contents," Sequence becomes "Chapter"), "Podcast," "Song," "Lecture," and "Business plan." Each row also includes a small "Artist Rendering" image featuring the 'AWESOM-O' robot. This slide directly expands on the generalization mentioned in Slide 2 and shows how the modular, hierarchical approach successfully addresses the "one-shot" long-form writing challenge across diverse document types.

This slide, titled "Coding Session," serves as a practical call to action or summary, consolidating the concepts introduced throughout the presentation. It presents three bullet points outlining key objectives: "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," and "Modify Awesome-O for different document types." The slide visually reinforces these objectives with a flowchart depicting the hierarchical "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes" stages, mirroring the "Screenplay Flow" from Slide 5. Crucially, small 'AWESOM-O' robot icons are strategically placed as connectors between each stage. These icons visually represent the integration of enhancement agents, as discussed in Slide 16, which refine the intermediate JSON outputs. This slide effectively ties together the core narrative of AI screenplay creation, its refinement through enhancement agents, and the generalization of the framework to other document types, building directly on Slide 17.
